other_observers: Bill Kaempfer saw this bird first on the roadway.;
duration: Seen well for about 20 seconds and then followed in flight for another 30 seconds
specific_location: On Oxford Road halfway between 115 and 119 streets.
description: I did not see this bird as well as Bill Kaempfer. I got onto it just as it was taking off. The bird was in a flock of Horned Larks, McCown's Longspurs and Chestnut-collared Longspurs. It was the same size and shape as the other Longspurs. It appeared to be of mostly uniform plumage, that I would describe as straw colored with not very distinguishing marks.  However, the tail had white outer tail feathers: I believe there were two each side as the white was very clearly visible. The tail pattern and uniform color was in contrast to the other longspurs present. Later I saw a Lapland Longspur, which was clearly patterned with rufous in the wing, some black in the throat, and a white belly with obvious side streaks.Behaviors: In a roadside with Horned Larks and other Longspurs when deep snow covered the fields. It took off from in front of of me, flew to my side and then went for several hundred yards behind me.Call: None heardPlumage: Not breeding male
